planting.note: Sow only after soil has warmed thoroughly; cold soil rots seed.

directions: Provide a sturdy 7-8 ft trellis before sowing, as vines climb aggressively and produce deep burgundy pods up to 18 inches long. Direct sow two weeks after last frost when soil reaches at least 70 F, planting seeds 1 inch deep. Harvest pods young and tender at pencil thickness for best flavor and to keep plants productive. Pick every two to three days during peak season to prevent pods from becoming fibrous. Feed lightly with a balanced fertilizer since heavy nitrogen reduces pod set.

planting.note: Bush habit needs no support; sow after frost danger passes.

directions: This Dutch heirloom wax bean produces buttery yellow pods streaked with purple that fade when cooked. Direct sow 1 inch deep in warm soil, spacing seeds 4 inches apart in rows 18 inches apart. Harvest as a snap bean at 5-6 inches or leave to mature for a fine shell bean. Pods are stringless and tender when picked young, so gather every few days. Avoid working among wet plants to reduce spread of bacterial blight.

planting.note: Tolerates cooler soil than common beans but still frost tender.

directions: White-flowered runner bean prized for its large white seeds and vigorous ornamental vines. Sow seeds 1.5 inches deep at the base of a tall trellis or teepee once soil warms to 60 F. Keep soil evenly moist during flowering to ensure good pod set, as heat above 90 F can cause blossom drop. Harvest young pods for snap use or allow to dry on the vine for shell beans. In mild climates the fleshy roots may overwinter and resprout the following spring.

planting.note: Grown mainly as a dry bean; needs full season to mature seed.

directions: Also called orca or calypso bean, this dry bush bean bears striking black-and-white seeds resembling the yin-yang symbol. Direct sow 1 inch deep after frost danger passes, spacing 4 inches apart in rows. Allow pods to dry fully on the plant until they rattle before harvesting. Pull whole plants and cure them in a dry, airy spot if wet weather threatens the maturing crop. Reduce watering as pods fill to encourage even drying and prevent mold.

planting.note: Sow as soon as soil can be worked; succession sow through summer.

directions: This Italian heirloom reveals concentric pink-and-white rings when sliced, and the rings show best when eaten raw or lightly cooked. Sow the multigerm seed clusters half an inch deep and thin seedlings to 3 inches once true leaves appear. Keep soil consistently moist to prevent woody, zoned roots and to encourage tender growth. Harvest when roots reach 1.5 to 2 inches across for the sweetest flavor. The greens are also edible and can be cooked like chard.

planting.note: Germination can be erratic; sow a bit thicker to compensate.

directions: Golden beets offer a mild, sweet flavor without the earthy note of red types and do not bleed when cut. Because germination is often spotty, presoak seed for a few hours and sow slightly denser than usual. Thin ruthlessly to 3 inches so roots can size up without crowding. Keep beds weed-free and evenly watered to avoid tough, cracked roots. Harvest young for salads or at full size for roasting.

planting.note: Long roots grow partly above soil; hill lightly to keep them covered.

directions: This Danish heirloom produces long cylindrical roots that yield uniform slices, ideal for pickling and canning. Sow seed half an inch deep in loose, stone-free soil so the elongated roots can develop straight. Thin to 3 inches and keep the beds evenly moist to prevent zoning and cracking. As roots push upward, draw a little soil over exposed shoulders to prevent greening. Harvest when roots reach about 6 inches long and 2 inches thick.

planting.note: Overwintering type; sow midsummer for spring harvest in mild zones.

directions: This hardy overwintering broccoli produces many small purple spears in early spring rather than a single central head. Start seed indoors in mid to late summer and transplant in early fall so plants establish before cold sets in. Provide steady moisture and rich soil, and mulch heavily to protect crowns through winter in zones 6 and 7. Harvest spears when buds are tight and cut regularly to encourage continued side-shoot production. Exposure to winter chill is what triggers the abundant spring flush.

planting.note: Flavor sweetens after frost; also spring-sow for summer harvest.

directions: Also known as Tuscan or lacinato kale, this Italian heirloom bears puckered blue-black strap leaves used in soups and braises. Start indoors and transplant, or direct sow, spacing plants 15 inches apart in fertile soil. Harvest lower leaves first, working upward and leaving the growing crown intact for continuous production. Cold weather concentrates sugars, so the best-flavored leaves come after the first hard frosts. Watch for cabbage worms in warm spells and cover with row fabric if needed.

planting.note: Tender enough for baby-leaf salad harvest as well as mature use.

directions: This oleracea-type kale has flat, frilly gray-green leaves with purple stems and is notably tender and sweet. Direct sow half an inch deep and thin to 12 inches, or harvest thinnings as baby greens. It tolerates both heat and cold better than curly kale, making it a good four-season crop. Snap off outer leaves as needed and the plant will keep pushing new growth from the center. A light frost improves its mild, sweet flavor.

planting.note: Consistent cool weather prevents buttoning; avoid heat stress.

directions: The vivid purple curds come from anthocyanins and do not require blanching like white types. Start seed indoors five to six weeks before transplanting and set plants out in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Keep growth steady and unchecked, since any stress from heat, drought, or transplant shock can cause premature small heads. Harvest while the head is still tight and dome-shaped, before the curds begin to separate. The color softens somewhat when cooked but stays vibrant if steamed briefly.

planting.note: Needs warm nights above 60 F to set fruit reliably.

directions: This Italian type bears glossy fruit striped in purple and white that fades to solid purple as it matures. Start seed indoors eight weeks before the last frost and transplant only after nights stay reliably above 60 F. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and stake plants that become top-heavy with fruit. Harvest while the skin is still glossy for the mildest, least seedy flesh. Consistent picking encourages the plant to keep flowering and fruiting.

planting.note: Earlier and more productive than large globe types in cool summers.

directions: Long, slender, thin-skinned fruit with tender flesh that cooks quickly and rarely needs peeling. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant into black plastic mulch to boost soil temperature in cooler regions. Feed with a balanced fertilizer and keep evenly watered to prevent bitter, seedy fruit. Cut fruit with pruners while young and glossy, typically at 6 to 8 inches long. These plants often outyield globe eggplants where summers are short or mild.

planting.note: Heat-loving Spanish heirloom; mulch to retain warmth and moisture.

directions: A Spanish heirloom with teardrop fruit striped purple and white and a notably sweet, mild flavor. Give it a long warm season, starting seed indoors early and transplanting into the warmest bed available. Fruit skin is thin and bruises easily, so handle carefully and harvest with shears. Pick when fruit is 5 to 7 inches and still shiny, before seeds harden. Steady watering prevents blossom-end problems and keeps flesh creamy.

planting.note: Choose day-length-appropriate strain for your latitude.

directions: These flat, disc-shaped Italian onions are prized for roasting whole and caramelizing due to their high sugar content. Start seed indoors ten weeks before last frost and transplant seedlings when pencil-thick. Keep beds weed-free since onions compete poorly, and water steadily until tops begin to fall. Stop watering once necks soften and leaves yellow, then lift and cure in a dry airy place. Well-cured bulbs store for several months in a cool spot.

planting.note: Long-day Italian type; best where summers have long daylight.

directions: This elongated torpedo-shaped Italian onion has a mild, sweet flavor ideal for fresh eating and grilling. Start indoors and transplant early, giving each bulb room to elongate in loose, fertile soil. Maintain even moisture through bulbing to develop full size, then taper off as tops lodge. Because it is a fresh-use onion, it stores only a few weeks compared with storage types. Harvest when most tops have fallen and cure briefly before use.

planting.note: Sow thickly and do not thin; crowding keeps bulbs small.

directions: Tiny pearl onions are grown deliberately crowded so each bulb stays small and round for pickling and boiling. Direct sow densely in a wide band and skip thinning, since competition limits bulb size. Keep the bed evenly moist and weed-free during the short growing period. Harvest when tops yellow and fall, then cure the little bulbs before storing or pickling. Blanching the cured bulbs in hot water makes peeling far easier.

planting.note: Plant sets pointed tip up with the neck just above soil.

directions: A large elongated shallot combining the size of an onion with a shallot's refined flavor. Plant individual sets in early spring with the tip barely showing, spacing them 6 inches apart. Unlike clustering shallots, banana shallots often size up as a single large bulb, so give each room. Keep weeded and evenly watered until tops begin to yellow in midsummer. Lift, cure thoroughly, and store the papery bulbs in a cool dry place for months.

planting.note: Very winter-hardy; can stand in the ground for winter harvest.

directions: This hardy Scottish heirloom leek withstands hard freezes and can be harvested through winter in many climates. Start seed indoors early, then transplant seedlings into deep dibble holes so the shanks blanch white. Hill soil around the stems or drop plants into holes to lengthen the tender white portion. Keep well watered and side-dress with nitrogen midseason for thick shanks. Dig leeks as needed all winter, lifting the last before spring regrowth begins.

planting.note: Keep seedbed constantly moist during slow germination.

directions: These anthocyanin-rich carrots range from purple skin with orange cores to solid purple throughout. Sow the fine seed a quarter inch deep in deep, loose, stone-free soil and never let the surface dry during germination. Thin seedlings to 2 to 3 inches so roots develop without forking or crowding. Purple pigment intensifies in cool weather, so fall crops often show the deepest color. Harvest when shoulders reach three-quarters of an inch and store in cool, humid conditions.

planting.note: Round roots tolerate heavy or shallow soils where long types fail.

directions: This round French heirloom carrot forms golf-ball-sized roots that thrive in clay or rocky ground where long carrots struggle. Sow seed thinly and shallowly, keeping the surface damp until the slow seedlings emerge. Thin to 2 inches to allow the little globes to plump up evenly. Because roots are short, they mature quickly and are excellent for containers. Harvest young at about 1 to 1.5 inches across for the sweetest, most tender roots.

planting.note: Deep sandy loam gives the straightest, smoothest roots.

directions: A creamy white carrot with crisp, sweet, mild flesh and no pigment, popular in mixed rainbow bunches. Sow shallowly in deeply worked sandy loam and maintain constant surface moisture until emergence. Thin carefully to prevent crowding, which causes twisted or stunted roots. Keep beds weeded early since carrot seedlings are easily overtaken. Harvest at full size, wash gently, and store in cold, humid conditions to retain crispness.

planting.note: Botanically a melon; needs heat and a long warm season.

directions: Though eaten like a cucumber, this is actually a ribbed, pale-green melon with tender skin that never needs peeling. Direct sow in warm soil after frost or start in pots, and trellis the long vines to keep fruit straight. Provide steady moisture and full sun for the sweetest, most tender fruit. Harvest at 12 to 18 inches while still slender, since oversized fruit turns seedy. Regular picking keeps the vine setting new fruit through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil; seedlings resent cold, wet conditions.

directions: This heirloom bears round, yellow, lemon-sized fruit with sweet, mild, non-bitter flesh. Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and grow on a trellis to keep the round fruit clean and easy to spot. Keep evenly watered to avoid bitterness and misshapen fruit during dry spells. Harvest while the skin is still pale yellow and before it turns deep gold and seedy. Frequent picking keeps the prolific vines producing until frost.

planting.note: Pickling type; succession sow for a steady supply.

directions: Kirby is the classic short, bumpy, thin-skinned pickling cucumber with crisp, dense flesh. Direct sow after the soil warms, either in hills or along a low trellis for cleaner fruit. Provide abundant, even water since pickling cucumbers are especially prone to bitterness and hollow centers under drought. Pick daily at 3 to 5 inches for pickling, as the vines produce heavily over a short window. Succession sowing every two to three weeks extends the harvest into late summer.

planting.note: Needs consistent heat; use black mulch in cooler climates.

directions: This intensely fragrant French melon has smooth gray-green skin and deep orange, aromatic flesh. Start seed indoors three weeks before transplanting to gain a head start in short-season areas, handling the sensitive roots gently. Grow on black plastic mulch with row cover early to trap warmth, removing cover at flowering for pollinators. Reduce watering as fruit ripens to concentrate sugars. Harvest when the fruit turns yellowish, smells sweet, and slips easily from the vine.

planting.note: Compact icebox type suited to shorter seasons and small gardens.

directions: This compact icebox watermelon produces round 8 to 10 pound fruit with sweet red flesh on shorter vines. Start seed indoors or direct sow once soil is thoroughly warm, and use black mulch in cool regions. Water steadily during vine growth and fruit set, then ease off as melons ripen to intensify sweetness. Signs of ripeness include a yellowing ground spot and a dried tendril nearest the fruit. Because it matures relatively quickly, it succeeds even where summers are short.

planting.note: Indian heirloom; heat-loving and quick to ripen.

directions: A striking Indian heirloom melon striped in green, orange, and cream with sweet, pale flesh. Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil once nights stay warm. Train vines with room to sprawl and mulch to keep fruit clean and warm. The dramatic rind color develops as the fruit ripens, and mature melons detach easily from the stem. Cut back on water near harvest to concentrate the sugars in the flesh.

planting.note: Long-season winter squash; some resistance to vine borers.

directions: This heirloom winter squash bears large crook-necked fruit with sweet flesh traditional in Southern pies. Direct sow several seeds per hill in warm soil, thinning to the two strongest plants. Give the vigorous vines ample room to ramble, as they can run 12 feet or more. Cushaws show useful resistance to squash vine borers compared with pumpkin types. Harvest when the rind hardens and the vine dies back, then cure in a warm spot to sweeten and improve storage.

planting.note: Eat young as summer squash or mature as winter squash.

directions: This Italian moschata squash produces long curved fruit eaten green like zucchini or ripened tan for winter storage. Direct sow in warm soil and trellis the vigorous climbing vines to save space and keep fruit straight. Harvested young at 8 to 12 inches, the dense flesh has few seeds and resists getting mushy when cooked. Its moschata parentage gives it strong resistance to squash vine borers. Left to ripen fully, the mature fruit stores for months like butternut.

planting.note: Ribbed Italian heirloom prized for flavor over sheer yield.

directions: This ribbed Italian heirloom zucchini has firm, richly flavored flesh and produces attractive star-shaped slices. Direct sow in warm, fertile soil and give the large, somewhat sprawling plants plenty of room. It yields fewer fruit than hybrids but each has superior nutty flavor and holds up well when grilled. Its abundant male blossoms are excellent for stuffing and frying. Harvest fruit young at 6 to 8 inches for the best texture and to keep production going.

planting.note: Bolts in heat; best as a spring and fall crop.

directions: This tender Asian green forms loose, frilly, pale chartreuse leaves used raw in salads or lightly cooked. Direct sow shallowly and thin to about 8 inches, or harvest thinnings as baby greens. It grows very fast and prefers cool weather, bolting quickly once temperatures climb. Keep soil evenly moist to maintain the crisp, mild leaves and prevent bitterness. Sow in early spring and again in late summer for tender harvests bracketing the heat of summer.

planting.note: Harvest flowering stems just as buds open for best flavor.

directions: Grown for its tender flowering stems, yu choy is a fast staple of Cantonese cooking with a mild, sweet flavor. Direct sow and thin to about 4 inches, keeping the soil consistently moist for quick, succulent growth. Harvest the main stem with a few leaves and a cluster of yellow buds when the first flowers just begin to open. Cutting the central stalk encourages tender side shoots for a second picking. Grow in cool weather to avoid the bitterness and toughness that come with heat.

planting.note: Sow in cool soil; germination fails above 75 F.

directions: This large French heirloom spinach produces enormous smooth leaves and is known for fast, heavy yields. Sow seed half an inch deep in cool soil as early as it can be worked, since heat prevents germination and triggers bolting. Thin to 6 inches and keep beds evenly moist and rich in nitrogen for lush leaves. Harvest whole plants or pick outer leaves for a cut-and-come-again supply. Sow again in late summer for a fall crop that sweetens with frost.

planting.note: Heat-loving green; thrives in the hottest part of summer.

directions: Also called Egyptian spinach or jute mallow, molokhia is a heat-loving green that thrives when other leafy crops bolt. Direct sow after the soil is thoroughly warm, since seedlings need heat and stall in cool weather. Pinch the growing tips to encourage bushy branching and a continuous supply of young leaves. Harvest the tender upper leaves and stems, which turn silky and thicken soups and stews when cooked. Keep it well watered through summer, and pick regularly to delay flowering.
